policy_statement: By 2025, report annually on gas decommissioning status including methane leakage reductions attributable to decommissioning/removal, and report on cost, equity, safety, and just transition (ES.5-2).
mechanism_description: The city will report annually on gas decommissioning progress and the methane leakage reductions attributed to decommissioning or removal of gas distribution, alongside cost, equity, safety, and just transition considerations.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance/utility action (decommission gas infrastructure + report progress) → ↓ fugitive methane leakage → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: The city/utility establishes annual reporting on gas decommissioning progress and explicitly quantifies methane leakage reductions attributable to decommissioning/removal (with associated cost/equity/safety/transition metrics).  
2) Mediating physical process: Decommissioning and removal reduces or eliminates pressurized gas distribution assets and associated leaks (fugitive emissions) from pipes, fittings, services, and related equipment; reporting improves oversight and prioritization of high-leak segments and accelerates follow-through.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower methane emissions (and associated CO₂e) from the local gas distribution system, contributing to overall GHG mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced fugitive methane emissions from the gas distribution system by driving and verifying progress on decommissioning/removal.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s climate impact centers on reducing fugitive methane emissions via gas infrastructure decommissioning, with reporting serving as the accountability/verification component.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While reporting is an “enabling” governance tool and could be framed as resource-management, the targeted outcome is explicitly methane leakage reduction (a direct GHG mitigation objective), not adaptation or efficiency of resource use.  
3) Edge cases applied: If reporting occurred without any actual decommissioning/leak reductions, the effect could be purely informational; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly ties reporting to decommissioning progress and attributed methane reductions, making mitigation the dominant intended effect.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy using this mechanism (reporting on decommissioning progress and methane leakage reductions attributable to it) is fundamentally oriented toward quantifying and supporting reductions in methane emissions from gas systems, i.e., mitigation.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether this is categorized as “fugitive emissions control,” “gas network retirement,” “MRV/accountability,” or “utility regulation” is deferred to policy-level evidence and implementation context.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be treated as an enabling/MRV-only action if it does not induce or accompany real-world decommissioning; however, because the mechanism specifies attribution of methane reductions to decommissioning/removal, the dominant pathway remains mitigation.
key_indicators: “By 2025, report annually”; “status of gas decommissioning”; “including reduction of methane leakage… attributable to decommissioning or removal”; “along with cost, equity, safety, and just transition”
co_benefits: Improved public safety oversight; equity/just transition accountability; cost transparency
instance_edge_case_notes: Although tied to a mitigation action (gas decommissioning), the stated instrument is reporting/measurement rather than a decommissioning mandate or performance standard; classification hinges on the explicit “report annually” requirement.
